title: Miss Edithן¿½s Modest Request
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My papa knows you, and he says youן¿½re a man who makes reading for books;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But I never read nothing you wrote, nor did Papa, I know by his looks.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So I guess youן¿½re like me when I talk, and I talk, and I talk all the day,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And they only say, ן¿½Do stop that child!ן¿½ or, ן¿½Nurse, take Miss Edith away.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But Papa said if I was good I could ask you alone by myself
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If you wouldnן¿½t write me a book like that little one up on the shelf.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I donן¿½t mean the pictures, of course, for to make them youן¿½ve got to be smart
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But the reading that runs all around them, you know, just the easiest part.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You neednן¿½t mind what itן¿½s about, for no one will see it but me,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Jane, thatן¿½s my nurse, and John, heן¿½s the coachman, just only us three.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Youן¿½re to write of a bad little girl, that was wicked and bold and all that;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And then youן¿½re to write, if you please, something good very good of a cat!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ This cat, she was virtuous and meek, and kind to her parents, and mild,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And careful and neat in her ways, though her mistress was such a bad child;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And hours she would sit and would gaze when her mistress thatן¿½s me was so bad,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And blink, just as if she would say, ן¿½Oh, Edith! you make my heart sad.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And yet, you would scarcely believe it, that beautiful, angelic cat
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was blamed by the servants for stealing whatever, they said, sheן¿½d get at.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And when John drank my milk, donן¿½t you tell me! I know just the way it was done,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They said ן¿½twas the cat, and she sitting and washing her face in the sun!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And then there was Dick, my canary. When I left its cage open one day,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They all made believe that she ate it, though I know that the bird flew away.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And why? Just because she was playing with a feather she found on the floor.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As if cats couldnן¿½t play with a feather without people thinking ן¿½twas more!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Why, once we were romping together, when I knocked down a vase from the shelf,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That cat was as grieved and distressed as if she had done it herself;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And she walked away sadly and hid herself, and never came out until tea,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So they say, for they sent me to bed, and she never came even to me.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No matter whatever happened, it was laid at the door of that cat.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Why, once when I tore my apron, she was wrapped in it, and I called ן¿½Rat!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Why, they blamed that on her. I shall never no, not to my dying day
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Forget the pained look that she gave me when they slapped me and took me away.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of course, you know just what comes next, when a child is as lovely as that:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She wasted quite slowly away; it was goodness was killing that cat.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I know it was nothing she ate, for her taste was exceedingly nice;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But they said she stole Bobbyן¿½s ice cream, and caught a bad cold from the ice.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And youן¿½ll promise to make me a book like that little one up on the shelf,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And youן¿½ll call her ן¿½Naomi,ן¿½ because itן¿½s a name that she just gave herself;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For sheן¿½d scratch at my door in the morning, and whenever Iן¿½d call out, ן¿½Whoן¿½s there?ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She would answer, ן¿½Naomi! Naomi!ן¿½ like a Christian, I vow and declare.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And youן¿½ll put me and her in a book. And mind, youן¿½re to say I was bad;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And I might have been badder than that but for the example I had.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And youן¿½ll say that she was a Maltese, and whatן¿½s that you asked? ן¿½Is she dead?ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Why, please, sir, there ainן¿½t any cat! Youן¿½re to make one up out of your head!
meaning:
This evocative piece by Bret Harte (Francis), titled "Miss Edithן¿½s Modest Request", represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
